๐งฌ DNA ME - CURSOR MASTER PROMPT v2.0\\\\\\\\n## Complete Custom CRM Platform with Smart Routing &amp; Intent Detection\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n**Tech Stack:** Node.js (Fastify) + PostgreSQL + Redis (BullMQ) + TypeScript  \\\\\\\\n**Timeline:** 6-8 weeks MVP\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n---\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n# ๐ QUICK SUMMARY\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nBuild a Marketing Automation CRM that:\\\\\\\\n1. **Ingests events** from 5+ channels (LinkedIn/Waalaxy, Email/Lemlist, Website, Ads, Conferences)\\\\\\\\n2. **Scores leads** in real-time with decay\\\\\\\\n3. **Detects product intent** (Research Lab vs B2B Lab vs Co-Creation)\\\\\\\\n4. **Routes leads** to correct pipeline based on score + intent\\\\\\\\n5. **Automates** stage movements and notifications\\\\\\\\n6. **Syncs** with Moco (German finance) and Slack (alerts)\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n---\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n# ๐จ CRITICAL: SMART ROUTING ARCHITECTURE\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n## The Problem\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nCold leads from scrapers have **NO product affinity yet**. We cannot assign them to a pipeline immediately.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n## The Solution: Global Lead Pool + Intent Detection\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\\\\\\\nโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโ\\\\\\\\nโ                         GLOBAL LEAD POOL (Stage 0)                           โ\\\\\\\\nโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโโค\\\\\\\\nโ                                                                             โ\\\\\\\\nโ  ALL NEW LEADS START HERE:                                                  โ\\\\\\\\nโ  Lead { pipeline_id: NULL, routing_status: 'unrouted', intent_signals: [] } โ\\\\\\\\nโ                                                                             โ\\\\\\\\nโ                              โผ                                              โ\\\\\\\\nโ                    Events accumulate                                        โ\\\\\\\\nโ                    Score increases                                          โ\\\\\\\\nโ                    Intent signals detected                                  โ\\\\\\\",\\\\\\\"signatures\\\\\\\":{},\\\\\\\"detailedLines\\\\\\\":[{\\\\\\\"text\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"# ๐งฌ DNA ME - CURSOR MASTER PROMPT v2.0\\\\\\\",\\\\\\\"lineNumber\\\\\\\":1},{\\\\\\\"text\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"## Complete Custom CRM Platform with Smart Routing &amp; details\\\\\\\\ncurl http://localhost:3000/api/v1/leads/&lt;lead-id&gt; \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\n  -H \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"X-API-Key: portal:key2\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"\\\\\\\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n## Database Schema\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThe CRM uses the following main tables:\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n| Table | Description |\\\\\\\\n|-------|-------------|\\\\\\\\n| &#96;leads&#96; | Contact information, scores, and routing status |\\\\\\\\n| &#96;organizations&#96; | Company data linked to leads |\\\\\\\\n| &#96;pipelines&#96; | Sales pipelines (Research, B2B, Co-Creation, Discovery) |\\\\\\\\n| &#96;pipeline_stages&#96; | Stages within each pipeline |\\\\\\\\n| &#96;deals&#96; | Opportunities linked to leads and pipelines |\\\\\\\\n| &#96;events&#96; | Marketing events (partitioned by month) |\\\\\\\\n| &#96;intent_signals&#96; | Detected intent signals for leads |\\\\\\\\n| &#96;scoring_rules&#96; | Configurable score calculation rules |\\\\\\\\n| &#96;score_history&#96; | Historical score changes with decay |\\\\\\\\n| &#96;automation_rules&#96; | Automation triggers and actions |\\\\\\\\n| &#96;tasks&#96; | Follow-up tasks for team members |\\\\\\\\n| &#96;team_members&#96; | Sales team members with capacity |\\\\\\\\n| &#96;campaigns&#96; | Marketing campaign tracking |\\\\\\\\n| &#96;api_keys&#96; | Webhook authentication keys |\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n## Docker Deployment\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n### Development\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\\\\\\\\ndocker-compose up -d\\\\\\\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n### Production\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\\\\\\\\ndocker-compose -f docker-compose.yml up -d api workers\\\\\\\\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n## Integrations\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n### Moco (German Finance)\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThe Moco integration enables:\\\\\\\\n- Customer creation when deals are won\\\\\\\\n- Offer/quote generation\\\\\\\\n- Invoice creation from offers\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nConfigure by setting &#96;MOCO_API_KEY&#96; and &#96;MOCO_SUBDOMAIN&#96; in your &#96;.env&#96; file.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n### Slack\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThe Slack integration provides:\\\\\\\\n- Hot lead alerts (#hot-leads)\\\\\\\",\\\\\\\"signatures\\\\\\\":{},\\\\\\\"detailedLines\\\\\\\":[{\\\\\\\"text\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"chmod +x class DealService {\\\\\\\\nasync searchDeals(filters: DealFiltersInput): Promise&lt;PaginatedResponse&lt;DealWithRelations&gt;&gt; {\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n    \\\\\\\\n    // Calculate pagination\\\\\\\\n    const page = filters.page || 1;\\\\\\\\n    const limit = filters.limit || 20;\\\\\\\\n    const offset = (page - 1) * limit;\\\\\\\\n    const totalPages = Math.ceil(total / limit);\\\\\\\\n    \\\\\\\\n    // Build order clause\\\\\\\\n    const validSortColumns = ['created_at', 'updated_at', 'value', 'name', 'stage_entered_at', 'position'];\\\\\\\\n    const sortBy = validSortColumns.includes(filters.sort_by || '') ? filters.sort_by : 'created_at';\\\\\\\\n    const sortOrder = (filters.sort_order || 'desc').toUpperCase();\\\\\\\\n    const orderClause = &#96;ORDER BY d.${sortBy} ${sortOrder} NULLS LAST&#96;;\\\\\\\\n    \\\\\\\\n    // Get data with relations\\\\\\\\n    const dataSql = &#96;\\\\\\\\n      SELECT \\\\\\\\n        d.*,\\\\\\\\n        p.name as pipeline_name,\\\\\\\\n        ps.name as stage_name,\\\\\\\\n        l.first_name as lead_first_name,\\\\\\\\n        l.last_name as lead_last_name,\\\\\\\\n        l.email as lead_email,\\\\\\\\n        CONCAT_WS(' ', l.first_name, l.last_name) as lead_name,\\\\\\\\n        o.name as lead_company,\\\\\\\\n        tm.name as assigned_to_name,\\\\\\\\n        seq.sequence_enrollment_id,\\\\\\\\n        seq.sequence_id,\\\\\\\\n        seq.sequence_name,\\\\\\\\n        seq.sequence_status,\\\\\\\\n        seq.sequence_current_step,\\\\\\\\n        seq.sequence_steps_total,\\\\\\\\n        seq.sequence_next_email_due_at,\\\\\\\\n        seq.sequence_stage_id\\\\\\\\n      FROM deals d\\\\\\\\n      JOIN pipelines p ON p.id = d.pipeline_id\\\\\\\\n      JOIN pipeline_stages ps ON ps.id = d.stage_id\\\\\\\\n      LEFT JOIN leads l ON l.id = d.lead_id\\\\\\\\n      LEFT JOIN organizations o ON o.id = l.organization_id\\\\\\\\n      LEFT JOIN team_members tm ON tm.email = d.assigned_to\\\\\\\\n      LEFT JOIN LATERAL (\\\\\\\\n        SELECT \\\\\\\\n          ese.id as sequence_enrollment_id,\\\\\\\\n          ese.sequence_id,\\\\\\\\n          ese.status as sequence_status,\\\\\\\\n          ese.current_step as sequence_current_step,\\\\\\\\n          ese.next_email_due_at as sequence_next_email_due_at,\\\\\\\\n          ese.stage_id as sequence_stage_id,\\\\\\\\n          es.name as sequence_name,\\\\\\\\n          (SELECT COUNT(*)::text FROM email_sequence_steps ess WHERE ess.sequence_id = ese.sequence_id) as sequence_steps_total\\\\\\\\n        FROM email_sequence_enrollments ese\\\\\\\\n        JOIN email_sequences es ON es.id = ese.sequence_id\\\\\\\\n        WHERE ese.deal_id = d.id AND ese.status = 'active'\\\\\\\\n        ORDER BY ese.enrolled_at DESC\\\\\\\\n        LIMIT 1\\\\\\\\n      ) seq ON true\\\\\\\\n      ${whereClause}\\\\\\\\n      ${orderClause}\\\\\\\\n      LIMIT $${paramIndex++} OFFSET $\\\\\\\\n&#96;;\\\\\\\\n  }\\\\\\\\n}\\\\\\\",\\\\\\\"signatures\\\\\\\":{\\\\\\\"ranges\\\\\\\":[{\\\\\\\"startPosition\\\\\\\":{\\\\\\\"line\\\\\\\":101,\\\\\\\"column\\\\\\\":1},\\\\\\\"endPosition\\\\\\\":{\\\\\\\"line\\\\\\\":101,\\\\\\\"column\\\\\\\":8}},{\\\\\\\"startPosition\\\\\\\":{\\\\\\\"line\\\\\\\":101,\\\\\\\"column\\\\\\\":8},\\\\\\\"endPosition\\\\\\\":{\\\\\\\"line\\\\\\\":102,\\\\\\\"column\\\\\\\":3}},{\\\\\\\"startPosition\\\\\\\":{\\\\\\\"line\\\\\\\":332,\\\\\\\"column\\\\\\\":3},\\\\\\\"endPosition\\\\\\\":{\\\\\\\"line\\\\\\\":333,\\\\\\\"column\\\\\\\":5}}]},\\\\\\\"detailedLines\\\\\\\":[{\\\\\\\"text\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"export spaceUris\\\":[],\\\"fileStates\\\":{},\\\"summaryArchives\\\":[],\\\"turnTimings\\\":[],\\\"fileStatesV2\\\":{},\\\"subagentStates\\\":{},\\\"selfSummaryCount\\\":0,\\\"readPaths\\\":[]},\\\"text\\\":\\\"\\\",\\\"toolFormerData\\\":{\\\"toolCallId\\\":\\\"tool_09e5b3be-975c-498e-923d-ca2e744a42b\\\",\\\"toolIndex\\\":0,\\\"modelCallId\\\":\\\"tool_09e5b3be-975c-498e-923d-ca2e744a42b\\\",\\\"status\\\":\\\"completed\\\",\\\"name\\\":\\\"read_file_v2\\\",\\\"rawArgs\\\":\\\"{\\\\\\\"path\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"e:\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\dname\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\crm_dna-me\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\src\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\db\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\index.ts\\\\\\\"}\\\",\\\"tool\\\":40,\\\"params\\\":\\\"{\\\\\\\"targetFile\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"e:\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\dname\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\crm_dna-me\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\src\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\db\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\index.ts\\\\\\\",\\\\\\\"charsLimit\\\\\\\":1000000,\\\\\\\"effectiveUri\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"e:\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\dname\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\crm_dna-me\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\src\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\db\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\index.ts\\\\\\\"}\\\",\\\"result\\\":\\\"{\\\\\\\"contents\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"// =============================================================================\\\\\\\\n// src/db/index.ts\\\\\\\\n// PostgreSQL Database Connection Pool\\\\\\\\n// =============================================================================\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nimport pg from 'pg';\\\\\\\\nimport { config } from '../config/index.js';\\\\\\\\nimport { DatabaseError } from '../errors/index.js';\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nconst { Pool } = pg;\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n// =============================================================================\\\\\\\\n// Pool Configuration\\\\\\\\n// =============================================================================\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nconst poolConfig: pg.PoolConfig = {\\\\\\\\n  connectionString: config.databaseUrl,\\\\\\\\n  max: 20,                    // Maximum connections in pool\\\\\\\\n  min: 2,                     // Minimum connections in pool\\\\\\\\n  idleTimeoutMillis: 30000,   // Close idle connections after 30s\\\\\\\\n  connectionTimeoutMillis: 5000, // Connection timeout\\\\\\\\n  allowExitOnIdle: false\\\\\\\\n};\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n// =============================================================================\\\\\\\\n// Create Pool\\\\\\\\n// =============================================================================\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nlet pool: pg.Pool | null = null;\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nexport function getPool(): pg.Pool {\\\\\\\\n  if (!pool) {\\\\\\\\n    pool = new Pool(poolConfig);\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n    pool.on('connect', () =&gt; {\\\\\\\\n      console.log('โ PostgreSQL client connected');\\\\\\\\n    });\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n    pool.on('error', (err) =&gt; {\\\\\\\\n      console.error('โ PostgreSQL pool error:', err.message);\\\\\\\\n    });\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n    pool.on('remove', () =&gt; {\\\\\\\\n      console.log('PostgreSQL client removed from pool');\\\\\\\\n    });\\\\\\\\n  }\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n  return pool;\\\\\\\\n}\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n// =============================================================================\\\\\\\\n// Database Helper Class\\\\\\\\n// =============================================================================\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nexport class Database {\\\\\\\\n  private pool: pg.Pool;\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n  constructor() {\\\\\\\\n    this.pool = getPool();\\\\\\\\n  }\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n  /**\\\\\\\\n   * Execute a query and return all rows\\\\\\\\n   */\\\\\\\\n  async query&lt;T = Record&lt;string, unknown&gt;&gt;(\\\\\\\\n    text: string,\\\\\\\\n    params?: unknown[]\\\\\\\\n  ): Promise&lt;T[]&gt; {\\\\\\\\n    try {\\\\\\\\n      const result = await this.pool.query(text, params);\\\\\\\\n      return result.rows as T[];\\\\\\\\n    } catch (error) {\\\\\\\\n      throw new DatabaseError(\\\\\\\\n        &#96;Query failed: ${(error as Error).message}&#96;,\\\\\\\\n        error as Error\\\\\\\\n      );\\\\\\\\n    }\\\\\\\\n  }\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n  /**\\\\\\\\n   * Execute a query and return the first row or null\\\\\\\\n   */\\\\\\\\n  async queryOne&lt;T = Record&lt;string, unknown&gt;&gt;(\\\\\\\\n    text: string,\\\\\\\\n    params?: unknown[]\\\\\\\\n  ): Promise&lt;T | null&gt; {\\\\\\\\n    const rows = await this.query&lt;T&gt;(text, params);\\\\\\\\n    return rows[0] ?? null;\\\\\\\\n  }\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n  /**\\\\\\\\n   * Execute a query and return the first row, throw if not found\\\\\\\\n   */\\\\\\\\n  async queryOneOrFail&lt;T = Record&lt;string, unknown&gt;&gt;(\\\\\\\\n    text: string,\\\\\\\\n    params?: unknown[],\\\\\\\\n    errorMessage: string = 'Record not found'\\\\\\\\n  ): Promise&lt;T&gt; {\\\\\\\\n    const row = await this.queryOne&lt;T&gt;(text, params);\\\\\\\\n    if (!row) {\\\\\\\\n      throw new DatabaseError(errorMessage);\\\\\\\\n    }\\\\\\\\n    return row;\\\\\\\\n  }\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n  /**\\\\\\\\n   * Execute a query and return the count of affected rows\\\\\\\\n   */\\\\\\\\n  async execute(text: string, params?: unknown[]): Promise&lt;number&gt; {\\\\\\\\n    try {\\\\\\\\n      const result = await this.pool.query(text, params);\\\\\\\\n      return result.rowCount ?? 0;\\\\\\\\n    } catch (error) {\\\\\\\\n      throw new DatabaseError(\\\\\\\\n        &#96;Execute failed: ${(error as Error).message}&#96;,\\\\\\\\n        error as Error\\\\\\\\n      );\\\\\\\\n    }\\\\\\\\n  }\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n  /**\\\\\\\\n   * Run a transaction with automatic rollback on error\\\\\\\\n   */\\\\\\\\n  async transaction&lt;T&gt;(\\\\\\\\n    callback: (client: pg.PoolClient) =&gt; Promise&lt;T&gt;\\\\\\\\n  ): Promise&lt;T&gt; {\\\\\\\\n    const client = await this.pool.connect();\\\\\\\\n    \\\\\\\\n    try {\\\\\\\\n      await client.query('BEGIN');\\\\\\\\n      const result = await callback(client);\\\\\\\\n      await client.query('COMMIT');\\\\\\\\n      return result;\\\\\\\\n    } catch (error) {\\\\\\\\n      await client.query('ROLLBACK');\\\\\\\\n      throw new DatabaseError(\\\\\\\\n        &#96;Transaction failed: ${(error as Error).message}&#96;,\\\\\\\\n        error as Error\\\\\\\\n      );\\\\\\\\n    } finally {\\\\\\\\n      client.release();\\\\\\\\n    }\\\\\\\\n  }\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n  /**\\\\\\\\n   * Check database connectivity\\\\\\\\n   */\\\\\\\\n  async healthCheck(): Promise&lt;boolean&gt; {\\\\\\\\n    try {\\\\\\\\n      const result = await this.queryOne&lt;{ now: Date }&gt;('SELECT NOW() as now');\\\\\\\\n      return result !== null;\\\\\\\\n    } catch {\\\\\\\\n      return false;\\\\\\\\n    }\\\\\\\\n  }\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n  /**\\\\\\\\n   * Get pool statistics\\\\\\\\n   */\\\\\\\\n  getStats() {\\\\\\\\n    return {\\\\\\\\n      totalCount: this.pool.totalCount,\\\\\\\\n      idleCount: this.pool.idleCount,\\\\\\\\n      waitingCount: this.pool.waitingCount\\\\\\\\n    };\\\\\\\\n  }\\\\\\\\n}\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n// =============================================================================\\\\\\\\n// Singleton Database Instance\\\\\\\\n// =============================================================================\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nlet dbInstance: Database | null = null;\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nexport function getDatabase(): Database {\\\\\\\\n  if (!dbInstance) {\\\\\\\\n    dbInstance = new Database();\\\\\\\\n  }\\\\\\\\n  return dbInstance;\\\\\\\\n}\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n// Convenience export\\\\\\\\nexport const db = {\\\\\\\\n  get instance() {\\\\\\\\n    return getDatabase();\\\\\\\\n  },\\\\\\\\n  query: &lt;T = Record&lt;string, unknown&gt;&gt;(text: string, params?: unknown[]) =&gt; \\\\\\\\n    getDatabase().query&lt;T&gt;(text, params),\\\\\\\\n  queryOne: &lt;T = Record&lt;string, unknown&gt;&gt;(text: string, params?: unknown[]) =&gt; \\\\\\\\n    getDatabase().queryOne&lt;T&gt;(text, params),\\\\\\\\n  queryOneOrFail: &lt;T = Record&lt;string, unknown&gt;&gt;(text: string, params?: unknown[], errorMessage?: string) =&gt; \\\\\\\\n    getDatabase().queryOneOrFail&lt;T&gt;(text, params, errorMessage),\\\\\\\\n  execute: (text: string, params?: unknown[]) =&gt; \\\\\\\\n    getDatabase().execute(text, params),\\\\\\\\n  transaction: &lt;T&gt;(callback: (client: pg.PoolClient) =&gt; Promise&lt;T&gt;) =&gt; \\\\\\\\n    getDatabase().transaction&lt;T&gt;(callback),\\\\\\\\n  healthCheck: () =&gt; \\\\\\\\n    getDatabase().healthCheck(),\\\\\\\\n  getStats: () =&gt; \\\\\\\\n    getDatabase().getStats()\\\\\\\\n};\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n// =============================================================================\\\\\\\\n// Close Pool\\\\\\\\n// =============================================================================\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nexport async function closePool(): Promise&lt;void&gt; {\\\\\\\\n  if (pool) {\\\\\\\\n    await pool.end();\\\\\\\\n    pool = null;\\\\\\\\n    dbInstance = null;\\\\\\\\n    console.log('PostgreSQL pool closed gracefully');\\\\\\\\n  }\\\\\\\\n}\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nexport default db;\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\",\\\\\\\"totalLinesInFile\\\\\\\":217}\\\"},\\\"capabilityType\\\":15}\",\"94e8b357-1f04-488e-80b5-3808b1e2e91f\":\"{\\\"_v\\\":3,\\\"type\\\":2,\\\"approximateLintErrors\\\":[],\\\"lints\\\":[],\\\"codebaseContextChunks\\\":[],\\\"commits\\\":[],\\\"pullRequests\\\":[],\\\"attachedCodeChunks\\\":[],\\\"assistantSuggestedDiffs\\\":[],\\\"gitDiffs\\\":[],\\\"interpreterResults\\\":[],\\\"images\\\":[],\\\"attachedFolders\\\":[],\\\"attachedFoldersNew\\\":[],\\\"bubbleId\\\":\\\"fd0cc1cd-9180-4f53-a943-7ed78ec9fd4a\\\",\\\"userResponsesToSuggestedCodeBlocks\\\":[],\\\"suggestedCodeBlocks\\\":[],\\\"diffsForCompressingFiles\\\":[],\\\"relevantFiles\\\":[],\\\"toolResults\\\":[],\\\"notepads\\\":[],\\\"capabilities\\\":[],\\\"multiFileLinterErrors\\\":[],\\\"diffHistories\\\":[],\\\"recentLocationsHistory\\\":[],\\\"recentlyViewedFiles\\\":[],\\\"isAgentic\\\":false,\\\"fileDiffTrajectories\\\":[],\\\"existedSubsequentTerminalCommand\\\":false,\\\"existedPreviousTerminalCommand\\\":false,\\\"docsReferences\\\":[],\\\"webReferences\\\":[],\\\"aiWebSearchResults\\\":[],\\\"requestId\\\":\\\"\\\",\\\"attachedFoldersListDirResults\\\":[],\\\"humanChanges\\\":[],\\\"attachedHumanChanges\\\":false,\\\"summarizedComposers\\\":[],\\\"cursorRules\\\":[],\\\"contextPieces\\\":[],\\\"editTrailContexts\\\":[],\\\"allThinkingBlocks\\\":[],\\\"diffsSinceLastApply\\\":[],\\\"deletedFiles\\\":[],\\\"supportedTools\\\":[],\\\"tokenCount\\\":{\\\"inputTokens\\\":0,\\\"outputTokens\\\":0},\\\"attachedFileCodeChunksMetadataOnly\\\":[],\\\"consoleLogs\\\":[],\\\"uiElementPicked\\\":[],\\\"isRefunded\\\":false,\\\"knowledgeItems\\\":[],\\\"documentationSelections\\\":[],\\\"externalLinks\\\":[],\\\"projectLayouts\\\":[],\\\"unifiedMode\\\":2,\\\"capabilityContexts\\\":[],\\\"todos\\\":[],\\\"createdAt\\\":\\\"2026-02-17T12:47:47.220Z\\\",\\\"mcpDescriptors\\\":[],\\\"workspaceUris\\\":[],\\\"conversationState\\\":{\\\"rootPromptMessagesJson\\\":[],\\\"turnsOld\\\":[],\\\" 2. Create test helper utilities (for mocking db, redis, etc.)
3. Create the actual integration test files:
   - Chat flow tests (conversation CRUD, message CRUD, full flow)
   - Email import tests (email matching, sync service logic)
   - Access control tests (conversation auth middleware, checkAccess)

I'll need to mock the database and Redis layers since there's no actual test database, so I'll set up Vitest's mocking capabilities to handle the `db` and `redis` modules. My plan is to create the vitest configuration, a shared test setup file with common mocks, and then the three integration test suites for chat flow, email import, and access control.

The key insight is that these are true integration tests because they exercise service-to-service interactions through the actual singleton imports, even though I'm mocking the external dependencies like the database and Redis. I'll mock the `db` and `redis` modules along with external services like nodemailer and IMAP, but let the services interact with each other naturally.

Now I'm writing the test suite...</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The error is clear: `vi.mock` factories are hoisted to the top of the file but they can't reference variables defined in the module scope because of hoisting. The error is:

```
Cannot access 'mockDb' before initialization
```

The solution is to use `vi.hoisted()` to declare the mock objects at the top, then use them in the factory functions. Alternatively, we can define the mocks inline in the factory functions, and access them via `vi.mocked()` or by defining them with `vi.hoisted`.

Let me restructure all three test files to use `vi.hoisted()`:

```typescript


Restructuring test files with hoisted mocks...
```</details></think>

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Good progress! 4 tests are failing in email-import.test.ts. The issues are:

1. **Strategy 2 tests** (Deal-ID in Subject): After the matchByDealId succeeds, the code continues to matchByActiveDeal which calls `db.query` (not `db.queryOne`), and since `mockDb.query` isn't set up for those tests, it returns `undefined`. I need to mock `mockDb.query` to return an empty array as fallback for the next strategies.

2. **Strategy 4 test** (Lead-Level Conversation): The mock sequence is wrong - after checking for active deals, it calls queryOne for lead conversation, but the mocks are consumed in wrong order.


3. **Strategy 5 test**: Same mock sequencing issue. I need to trace through the actual flow of `matchEmailToConversation` to understand what database calls happen in what order for each strategy, then set up the mocks accordingly.

For Strategy 2, the flow is: check `inReplyTo` (which is null, so skip), check `references` (empty array, so skip), then extract the deal ID from the subject and call `matchByDealId` which uses `db.queryOne`. The mock setup shows the first `queryOne` returns null for the in-reply-to check, and the second returns the conversation ID for the deal ID matchโbut I need to verify this matches the actual code path.

Looking at the test setup, since `inReplyTo` is null and `references` is empty, `matchByDealId` should be the first database call, so it should get the first mock value. I need to reorder the mocks so `matchByDealId` gets `{ id: convId }` on its first call, and then Strategy 4 uses `db.query` (not `queryOne`) which requires a separate mock setup.</details></think>

---

Gut, 4 Tests schlagen fehl. Die Probleme sind inkorrekte Mock-Sequenzen. Ich sehe, dass:
1. Bei Strategy-2-Tests wird `matchByInReplyTo` gar nicht aufgerufen (da `inReplyTo` null ist), also ist die Mock-Reihenfolge falsch
2. Strategy 3 verwendet `db.query` (nicht `queryOne`), das fehlt
3. Strategy 4 und 5 haben รคhnliche Sequenz-Probleme

Ich korrigiere die betroffenen Tests.

The `assigned_to` field in the `deals` table is likely a `VARCHAR` (storing email addresses), but it's being compared with a UUID (`userId`). Looking at the `ConversationService.checkAccess` method and `getConversations`, the query compares `d.assigned_to = $2` where `$2` is `userId` (a UUID), but `assigned_to` might store emails instead of UUIDs.

2. **400 Error**: `Invalid conversation data` - The create conversation endpoint is rejecting the request body.


I need to check the conversations route handler to see what validation is being applied, and then examine the deals table schema to confirm the data type of the `assigned_to` column.</details></think>

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Now I see the problems clearly:

1. **500 Error**: `deals.assigned_to` is `VARCHAR(255)` (stores email addresses like "agent@dna.de"), but the `checkAccess` and `getConversations` methods compare it with `userId` which is a UUID. The comparison `d.assigned_to = $2` where `$2` is a UUID fails because PostgreSQL cannot compare `VARCHAR` with `UUID` type.

2. **400 Error**: The frontend's create dialog sends `{ type: 'direct', subject: '...' }` without `lead_id` or `deal_id`. 

The Zod schema's `.refine()` requires at least one of them. For the first issue, I need to update the SQL queries in `conversationService.ts` to compare `d.assigned_to` against the user's email address instead of their UUID. Looking at the `checkAccess` method, I'll need to modify the query logic...

Fixing the SQL queries... I need to verify what value is actually stored in the `assigned_to` field by checking how deals are created and assigned throughout the codebase, since the schema shows it's a VARCHAR but I need to confirm whether it stores an email address or something else.

The real issue is that the query compares `d.assigned_to` (which stores emails as a string) against `$2` (a UUID), causing a type mismatch in PostgreSQL. I should either pass the user's email as a parameter instead of the UUID, or explicitly cast the comparison. The correct fix is to compare against the email since that's what's actually stored in that column.

I should also check if the `getConversations` method has the same problem, and for the 400 error on create, the frontend either needs to include a lead or deal ID in the payload, or we need to relax the validation for internal conversation types.

Looking at the schema constraint that requires at least one of `lead_id` or `deal_id`, but the frontend create dialog doesn't provide either option, I need to decide between fixing the frontend to include lead selection or allowing internal conversations without those fields. The quickest approach would be to make internal conversations exempt from that requirement and update the frontend accordingly.

But I'm realizing there's also a type mismatch issueโthe `assigned_to` field in deals appears to store an email string, but I'm comparing it against a UUID. Let me trace through how deals are actually assigned to understand whether `assigned_to` holds an email or an ID, then I can fix both the type comparison and the conversation creation flow.</details></think>

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Now I can see clearly:

1. In `automationEngine.ts` line 497: `SET assigned_to = $1 ... [owner.email, deal.id]` - So `assigned_to` stores the **email address** of the team member.

2. In `pipelineRouter.ts` line 254: `SET assigned_to = $1 ... [owner.email, dealId]` - Same pattern.

3. In `team.ts` line 656: `WHERE assigned_to = $1 ... [id]` - Here `id` is the team member ID (from the URL params), which would be a UUID. This is ALSO a bug but it might work if the `assigned_to` column actually contains UUIDs sometimes.
   
So the real problem is that `automationEngine.ts` is storing emails in `assigned_to`, but `team.ts` is querying with a UUID, which means the comparison silently returns zero results instead of matching anything. The `conversationService.ts` issue appears to be something different entirely.

Let me trace through the type inference here. The `user.id` from the JWT payload is technically a string in TypeScript, but when PostgreSQL sees `$2` being compared against a UUID column like `created_by_id`, it infers the parameter as UUID type. That's where the type mismatch error comes fromโthe database is trying to compare a UUID column with what it thinks is a UUID parameter, but the actual value being passed is a string. The real issue is that PostgreSQL infers `$2` as UUID from the first comparison, then fails when trying to use it as VARCHAR in the `assigned_to` check. I need to add the user email as a separate parameter so each comparison uses the correct type, and also handle the 400 error on create by either requiring `lead_id` or `deal_id` from the frontend, or relaxing the validation for internal conversations.

Updating the query parameters... I'm checking the database migration to see if there's a CHECK constraint that would prevent internal conversations without a lead or deal attached.</details></think>

---

Jetzt verstehe ich beide Probleme:

1. **500 Error**: `deals.assigned_to` ist `VARCHAR(255)` und speichert E-Mail-Adressen, aber die SQL-Query vergleicht mit `userId` (UUID). PostgreSQL kann `VARCHAR` nicht mit `UUID` vergleichen.

2. **400 Error**: Das Frontend-Create-Dialog sendet nur `{ type, subject }` ohne `lead_id` oder `deal_id`, aber die Zod-Validierung erfordert mindestens eines davon.

Lassen Sie mich die Migration und die Route prรผfen.

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Now I understand both problems completely:

**Problem 1: SQL Type Mismatch (500 error)**
In `conversationService.ts`, the `checkAccess` method and `getConversations` method compare `d.assigned_to = $userId` where:
- `d.assigned_to` is `VARCHAR(255)` (stores email like "agent@dna.de")
- `$userId` is being treated by PostgreSQL as UUID type (because `c.created_by_id = $2` in the same WHERE clause forces parameter $2 to be UUID typed)

Fix: Use `userEmail` for `assigned_to` comparisons instead of `userId`, since deals are assigned using email addresses.
 
**Problem 2: Create Conversation Validation (400 error)**
The database enforces that conversations must have either a lead or deal attached, but the frontend's create dialog is sending just a type and subject without either. I need to decide whether to relax the database constraint to allow internal conversations, modify the frontend to require selecting a lead or deal, or handle this differently in the backend.

The cleanest solution is to add a lead selector to the create dialogโI can use a simple text input where users type a lead email and the backend looks it up, or leverage the existing react-admin dataProvider to fetch available leads. I'm deciding between fixing the SQL constraint issue and adding lead search to the frontend, and I need to check what lead API endpoints are available to make this work.</details></think>

---

Jetzt habe ich beide Probleme genau identifiziert. Lassen Sie mich beide beheben:

**Problem 1**: `d.assigned_to` ist VARCHAR mit E-Mail-Adressen, wird aber mit UUID `userId` verglichen. PostgreSQL erkennt `$2` als UUID (wegen `c.created_by_id = $2`) und kann dann `VARCHAR = UUID` nicht vergleichen.

**Problem 2**: Das Create-Dialog sendet kein `lead_id`/`deal_id`, was aber durch die Zod-Validierung und DB-Constraint erforderlich ist.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

---

Die รnderungen sind gemacht. Hier ist eine Zusammenfassung der Fixes:

---

### Fix 1: SQL-Typkonflikt (500 Error)

**Ursache**: `deals.assigned_to` ist `VARCHAR(255)` und speichert E-Mail-Adressen (z.B. `"agent@dna.de"`). In den SQL-Queries wurde es aber mit `$userId` (UUID) verglichen. PostgreSQL konnte die Typen `VARCHAR` und `UUID` nicht direkt vergleichen.

**Lรถsung** in `conversationService.ts`:
- `checkAccess()`: Neuer Parameter `$4` = `userEmail` fรผr den Vergleich mit `d.assigned_to`
- `getConversations()`: Neuer Parameter `$paramIdx+2` = `userEmail` fรผr den Vergleich mit `d.assigned_to`

### Fix 2: Ungรผltige Konversationsdaten (400 Error)

**Ursache**: Das Create-Dialog hat nur `{ type, subject }` gesendet, aber die Zod-Validierung und die DB-Constraint (`chk_conversations_entity`) erfordern, dass mindestens `lead_id` oder `deal_id` gesetzt ist.

**Lรถsung** in `frontend/src/pages/Chat/index.jsx`:
- Ein **Lead-Autocomplete-Suchfeld** wurde hinzugefรผgt (mit 300ms Debounce)
- Der Nutzer sucht nach Lead-Name oder E-Mail, wรคhlt einen Lead aus
- Die `lead_id` wird dann beim Erstellen mitgesendet
- Der "Erstellen"-Button ist deaktiviert, solange kein Lead ausgewรคhlt ist

---

Bitte den Backend-Server neu starten (`npm run dev`), damit die รnderungen in `conversationService.ts` wirksam werden. Das Frontend wird per Hot-Reload automatisch aktualisiert.

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Now I see the issues. The `/read` and `/typing` routes use `checkConversationAccess` middleware which calls `conversationService.checkAccess`. With the fix I just made, the SQL should work now (using `userEmail` instead of `userId` for the `assigned_to` comparison). But the error is 400 Bad Request, not 403 Forbidden or 500 Internal Server Error. 

Wait - the errors are 400 Bad Request, not 403 or 500. This means the issue is different from the access control check. Let me look at what could cause a 400 error.

Actually, looking again at the routes:


Both `/read` and `/typing` validate the conversation ID parameter using a schema, and throw a ValidationError if the parameter doesn't match the expected format.

The conversation ID `411a6a4b-4136-45cd-a4f5-d40a1bb48843` is a valid UUID, so parameter validation shouldn't be the issue. I'm wondering if the 400 error is actually coming from Fastify's schema validation insteadโmaybe the routes have body schemas defined that are rejecting the requests. Looking at the frontend calls, `/read` sends a POST with no body, and `/typing` also doesn't appear to send a body, so if either route expects one, that could be triggering the error.</details></think>

---

Ich prรผfe den `httpClient` und den `MessageComposer`.

---

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="read_file_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **read_file_v2** โข Read file: e:\dname\crm_dna-me\frontend\src\providers\dataProvider.js</summary>

</details>
</tool-use>

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="read_file_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **read_file_v2** โข Read file: e:\dname\crm_dna-me\frontend\src\components\Chat\MessageComposer.jsx</summary>

</details>
</tool-use>

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Now I can see the problem. The `httpClient` always sets `Content-Type: application/json`, even for POST requests without a body. When Fastify receives a POST request with `Content-Type: application/json` but an empty body, it tries to parse the JSON and fails, resulting in a 400 Bad Request.

For the `/read` and `/typing` POST endpoints, the frontend sends:
```javascript
await httpClient(`${API_URL}/conversations/${conversationId}/read`, { method: 'POST' });
await httpClient(`${API_URL}/conversations/${conversationId}/typing`, { method: 'POST' });


```

These requests have no body but still include the JSON content type header, which Fastify interprets as a promise of valid JSON data. The simplest solution is to include an empty JSON object as the body for these requests, which satisfies Fastify's expectations. I need to update both the `/read` call in ChatWindow.jsx and the `/typing` call in MessageComposer.jsx to send `{}` as the body.

Now let me trace through the actual message sending flow more carefully. When a user submits a message, the frontend sends the message type, direction, and body content to the creation endpoint. Looking at the schema validation, the required fields should be present, but I'm seeing a 400 error on the `/read` endpoint which suggests the issue might be earlier in the request chain. Let me check what's actually happening when the user tries to send a message.

```

json
{
  "message_type": "email" or "internal_note",
  "direction": "internal" or "outbound",
  "body_text": "...",
  "body_html": "<p>...</p>"
}
```

The `/typing` endpoint is throwing 400 errors repeatedly, which is creating noise and might be masking the real issue with message creation. I should fix those validation errors first by ensuring the endpoints accept empty request bodies where appropriate, then trace through the message service to see if there's a validation issue preventing emails from actually being sent.</details></think>

Alle drei Fehler sind behoben. Hier die Zusammenfassung:

---

### Fix 1: 400 bei `/typing` und `/read` (Massen-Fehler)

**Ursache**: Der `httpClient` setzt immer `Content-Type: application/json`, aber die POST-Requests fรผr `/read`, `/typing` und `/retry` sendeten keinen Body. Fastify versucht dann den leeren Body als JSON zu parsen und gibt 400 zurรผck.

**Lรถsung**: In `ChatWindow.jsx` und `MessageComposer.jsx` wurde `body: JSON.stringify({})` zu allen body-losen POST-Requests hinzugefรผgt.

### Fix 2: E-Mail wird nicht gesendet

**Ursache**: Der `MessageComposer` sendet nur `{ message_type, direction, body_text, body_html }` ohne `recipients`. Die `sendEmailForMessage`-Methode prรผft `toAddresses.length === 0` und wirft einen Fehler.

**Lรถsung**: In `messageService.ts` wird jetzt automatisch:
- Der **Lead-E-Mail** als Empfรคnger gesetzt, wenn keine `recipients` angegeben werden (aus der Konversation โ Lead-Tabelle)
- Die **Absender-Info** aus den `team_members` รผbernommen, wenn `sender_email` fehlt

---

Bitte den Backend-Server neu starten, damit die รnderungen in `messageService.ts` und `conversationService.ts` wirksam werden.

---

_**User**_

ะฟะธััะผะพ ะฒัะต ัะฐะฒะฝะพ ะฝะต ะพััะฐะฒะปัะตััั ะธ ะฝะต ะฟะพะปััะฐะตััั ะฝะฐะทะฐะด. ะทะฝะฐัะพะบ ะพัะฟัะฐะฒะบะธ ะฟัะพั ัะพ ะบัััะธััั ะฝะฐ ะผะตััะต
Uncaught (in promise) The message port closed before a response was received.

---

_**Agent (model claude-4.6-opus-high-thinking, mode Agent)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The user says the email is still not being sent and the send spinner just keeps spinning. Let me investigate what's happening. The "message port closed" error is typically a browser extension issue, not related to our code.

The key issue is the spinning send button. Let me trace the flow:

1. User clicks send โ `handleSend` in MessageComposer calls `onSend(payload)`
2. `onSend` in ChatWindow calls `httpClient(POST /conversations/:id/messages, body)` 
3. The backend creates the message and tries to send email via SMTP

The spinner keeps going, which means the POST request is either hanging or failing. Let me check the backend server logs and also check if there's an issue with the SMTP sending that causes the request to hang.
 
I see the problem now. The `sendEmailForMessage` function is awaited inside `createMessage`, so if the SMTP send hangs or times out, the entire POST request hangs and never returns. The function tries to get recipients from the lead, then attempts to send via the user's SMTP account with a fallback to the system SMTP, but if either of those operations stalls, the request gets stuck. I need to check the email service implementation to see if there are timeout configurations or if the system SMTP is misconfigured. the `createMessage` flow calls `sendEmailForMessage` only for `direction === 'outbound' && !data.skip_send`. The error is caught inside `sendEmailForMessage`, so it shouldn't crash the request - it just sets the message status to 'error'.

But wait - what if the SMTP connection itself hangs? The `sendViaUserAccount` or `emailService.sendEmail` might hang indefinitely if there's no timeout configured, which would cause the HTTP request to never respond.

Let me look at the email service and check if there's proper timeout handling. Also, let me check what the system SMTP configuration looks like - if it's not configured, the connection attempt might hang.

Also, let me reconsider: maybe the issue is simpler. Perhaps the message IS being created in the DB, but the response isn't getting back to the frontend because of the SMTP send hanging.

The real solution here is probably to decouple email sending from the HTTP response - either by making it async or adding strict timeouts to the email service.</details></think>

---

Das Problem ist wahrscheinlich, dass der SMTP-Versand die HTTP-Antwort blockiert. Lassen Sie mich die E-Mail-Sendelogik und die SMTP-Konfiguration prรผfen.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Now I see the core problem. Looking at the flow:

1. Frontend sends POST to create message
2. Backend calls `createMessage` which:
   a. Inserts message in DB
   b. If outbound email: calls `await this.sendEmailForMessage(message, conversation, userId)` 
   c. This awaits the actual SMTP send before returning the response

The issue is that `sendEmailForMessage` is **synchronous** (awaited) - it tries to send the email via SMTP before returning the HTTP response. If:
- SMTP is not configured: `sendEmail` returns immediately with `{ success: false, error: 'SMTP nicht konfiguriert' }`. But then in `sendEmailForMessage`, the error is caught and the status is set to 'error'. This should work.


- SMTP is configured but the connection hangs: the request will hang indefinitely

Let me look more closely at the `sendEmailForMessage` implementation to understand the flow - it updates the message status to 'sending', then validates recipients and attempts to send through the user's account first.

If that fails, it falls back to the system SMTP service, and if that also fails it catches the error and marks the message as failed. So if SMTP isn't configured, the system SMTP call would fail and the error gets caught and logged properly.

But I'm realizing `sendViaUserAccount` could potentially hang indefinitely, which would block the entire send operation.</details></think>

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Now I understand the full picture. The problem is that `createMessage` awaits the entire email send process (including SMTP). This blocks the HTTP response. If SMTP is slow or not configured, the user waits.

Looking more carefully: If SMTP is not configured (`this.isConfigured === false`), the `sendEmail` method returns immediately with `{ success: false }`. Then `sendEmailForMessage` throws an error, which is caught, and the message status is set to 'error'. In that case, the response should come back relatively quickly.

But there's another issue: the response from `createMessage` returns the **original** message (from the INSERT), which has status `'draft'`. 

Let me trace through the actual flow in `createMessage` โ I need to see exactly when the message is inserted, when the status gets updated, and what gets returned to the client. So the issue is that `sendEmailForMessage` is blocking the entire request. When SMTP isn't configured, it should fail quickly through the database updates and email service checks, but something is causing the spinner to hang indefinitely. The user mentioned the send icon just keeps spinning, which suggests the frontend never receives a response. Let me trace through what's happening on the frontend side in the MessageComposer to see where the request might be getting stuck. I'm tracing through the message sending flowโthe composer triggers the send, which calls the ChatWindow handler that makes the HTTP POST request and waits for the response, then appends the message to the conversation. The issue is that if the HTTP request hangs or times out, the sending state stays locked because the await never resolves.

The root cause is that the backend's `createMessage` endpoint awaits the email sending operation, which itself awaits the SMTP connectionโso if SMTP is misconfigured or times out, the entire HTTP response gets blocked. The solution is to decouple email sending from the message creation response: return the message immediately after saving it to the database, then handle the email sending asynchronously in the background so it doesn't block the user's UI.</details></think>

---

Jetzt sehe ich das Kernproblem. Die `createMessage`-Methode **wartet** auf den SMTP-Versand (`await sendEmailForMessage()`), bevor sie die HTTP-Antwort zurรผckgibt. Wenn SMTP nicht konfiguriert ist oder die Verbindung hรคngt, blockiert das den gesamten Request und der Spinner dreht sich endlos.

Die Lรถsung: E-Mail-Versand asynchron machen (fire-and-forget), damit die Nachricht sofort zurรผckgegeben wird.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I see that the response sends the raw `message` object. The frontend in ChatWindow.jsx expects `json.data ?? json`:
```javascript
const { json } = await httpClient(
  `${API_URL}/conversations/${conversationId}/messages`,
  { method: 'POST', body: JSON.stringify(payload) }
);
const msg = json.data ?? json;
```

Since the route sends `reply.code(201).send(message)` (not wrapped in `{ data: ... }`), the frontend will get `json` = the message object, and `json.data` won't exist (unless the message object has a `data` field, which it doesn't), so `msg = json` = the message. That's fine.


Now that the SMTP send is fire-and-forget, I should verify the Zod schema for `createMessageSchema` matches what the frontend is sendingโchecking that fields like `message_type`, `direction`, `body_text`, and `body_html` are all properly defined.</details></think>

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="read_file_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **read_file_v2** โข Read file: e:\dname\crm_dna-me\src\services\messageService.ts</summary>

</tool-use>

---

Alles ist korrekt. Hier ist die Zusammenfassung der Fixes:

---

### Was behoben wurde

**Hauptproblem: Spinner dreht endlos beim Senden**

**Ursache**: `createMessage()` hat den SMTP-Versand **synchron** (`await sendEmailForMessage()`) abgewartet, bevor die HTTP-Antwort zurรผckgegeben wurde. Wenn der SMTP-Server langsam war, nicht erreichbar war, oder nicht konfiguriert war, blockierte das den gesamten Request โ der Spinner im Frontend drehte sich endlos.

**Lรถsung** (3 รnderungen):

1. **`messageService.ts` โ Fire-and-Forget E-Mail-Versand**: Der SMTP-Versand lรคuft jetzt **im Hintergrund** (nicht-blockierend). Die Nachricht wird sofort in die Datenbank geschrieben und als HTTP-Antwort zurรผckgegeben. Der tatsรคchliche SMTP-Versand aktualisiert den Status (`sending` โ `sent`/`error`) asynchron in der DB.

2. **`messageService.ts` โ Auto-Empfรคnger**: Wenn keine `recipients` angegeben werden, wird automatisch die E-Mail-Adresse des Leads aus der Konversation verwendet. Ebenso wird der Absender automatisch aus den `team_members` ermittelt.

3. **`ChatWindow.jsx` / `MessageComposer.jsx` โ Leerer JSON-Body**: Alle POST-Requests ohne Body (`/read`, `/typing`, `/retry`) senden jetzt `body: JSON.stringify({})`, um Fastifys JSON-Parser zufriedenzustellen.

---

Bitte den **Backend-Server** neu starten, damit die รnderungen wirksam werden.

---

_**User**_

message-status-sending ะบัััะธััั ะฝะฐ ะผะตััะต, ะฟะธััะผะพ ะฝะต ะฟัะธัะพะดะธั. 
Invalid login: 535 5.7.8 Error: authentication failed: (reason unavailable)

---

_**Agent (model claude-4.6-opus-high-thinking, mode Agent)**_

Das Problem ist klar: Die SMTP-Anmeldedaten sind falsch oder das Konto erlaubt keine App-Passwรถrter. Der Status bleibt auf "sending" hรคngen, weil die asynchrone Fehlerbehandlung den Status zwar auf "error" setzt, aber das Frontend die aktualisierte Nachricht nicht erneut abruft.

Ich behebe zwei Dinge:
1. Das Frontend soll den Status-Update nach dem Senden erkennen
2. Wenn SMTP fehlschlรคgt, soll die Nachricht trotzdem als "gesendet" (interne Notiz) gelten oder der Fehler sofort angezeigt werden

Lassen Sie mich erstmal den genauen Ablauf nachvollziehen.

---

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Now I understand the full picture:

1. **SMTP Authentication fails** โ `535 5.7.8 Error: authentication failed` โ The email can't be sent
2. `sendEmailForMessage` catches the error and calls `this.updateMessageStatus(message.id, 'error', errMsg)`
3. But the frontend only sees the initial message with `status: 'draft'` (returned immediately from `createMessage`)
4. The polling/SSE hook's `appendMessage` only **adds new messages** - it doesn't **update existing ones**
5. The `status` event handler in SSE could update messages, but we never publish a status update event from `updateMessageStatus`


6. The real fix is to publish a status change event via Redis when `updateMessageStatus` runs, and have the polling logic update existing messages instead of just appending new ones.</details></think>

---

Ich sehe zwei Probleme:

1. **SMTP-Authentifizierung fehlschlรคgt** โ die Anmeldedaten sind falsch
2. **Status bleibt auf "sending" hรคngen** โ der Backend-Code aktualisiert den Status in der DB auf "error", aber publiziert kein Event, das das Frontend informiert

Lassen Sie mich den `updateMessageStatus` und die Event-Publikation fixen.

---

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I see it now. The `publishMessageEvent` always publishes with `type: 'new_message'`. But the SSE frontend handler for messages uses `appendMessage` which only ADDS a new message (skips if ID already exists):

```javascript
es.addEventListener('message', (e) => {
  const payload = JSON.parse(e.data);
  const msg = payload.message ?? payload;
  if (msg && msg.id) appendMessage(msg);});
```

And `appendMessage`:
```javascript
const appendMessage = useCallback((msg) => {
  setMessages((prev) => {
    if (prev.some((m) => m.id === msg.id)) return prev;  // <-- SKIPS existing messages!
    return [...prev, msg];
  });}, []);
```

So when the status changes from 'draft' โ 'sending' โ 'error', the event is published as `type: 'new_message'` but `appendMessage` ignores it because the message ID already exists! The frontend has a `status` event handler that can update existing messages, but the backend never publishes a `status` event โ only `new_message`. I need to change `updateMessageStatus` to publish `type: 'message_status_update'` instead, and make sure the frontend handler processes those events to update the existing message's status.

Now I'm checking the SSE route handler to see how it converts Redis events into SSE messages.</details></think>

---

Ich habe das Problem gefunden. Der `publishMessageEvent` sendet immer `type: 'new_message'`, aber der Frontend-Code (`appendMessage`) ignoriert Nachrichten, die schon existieren. Die Status-Updates kommen nie an. Ich muss:

1. Einen separaten Status-Update-Event publizieren
2. Die SSE-Route muss diesen Event als `status`-Event weiterleiten

Lassen Sie mich die SSE-Route prรผfen.

---
